frontmatter.shortDescription: A value is data and may be the result of evaluating an expression or the contents of a variable.
Parameters of a callback function may be written as parameters of the caller function
o==p compares the objects referred to by variables o and p
The equality operator compares two objects' values
o.equals(p) compares the references stored in the variables o and p
One has to evaluate an expression to determine its type
To use a function as a value, one needs to have parentheses after its name
Local variables are automatically initialized
A variable can contain more than one value
Constructors must assign values to all fields
If a variable is at least as big (bit-width) as a value, then no cast is needed to a assign the value to the variable
Functions without return statements return no value at all
The values null and undefined are the same
null is an object
null is an object
Primitive values are heap objects
The type of a primitive variable depends on its value
The value null is of type 'null'
If a class has a method that has a local variable, parameter, or return value with the class as its type, the class is a recursive type
A method with void return type can return a value

frontmatter.title: Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools
frontmatter.author: Alfred Aho, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey Ullman, Monica S. Lam

frontmatter.title: nLab
frontmatter.author: David Corfield, David Roberts, Mike Shulman, Toby Bartels, Todd Trimble

pageContext.wikipediaSummary: In computer science, a value is the representation of some entity that can be manipulated by a program. The members of a type are the values of that type.The "value of a variable" is given by the corresponding mapping in the environment. In languages with assignable variables it becomes necessary to distinguish between the r-value (or contents) and the l-value (or location) of a variable.In declarative (high-level) languages, values have to be referentially transparent. This means that the resulting value is independent of the location in which a (sub-)expression needed to compute the value is stored. Only the contents of the location (the bits, whether they are 1 or 0) and their interpretation are significant.
